solution:
Force due to magnetic field on a current carrying conductor is given by, F→=il→×B→.

The angle between the magnetic field and the direction of the current in the wire CD is 60°. Therefore,

F=ilBsin60°⇒F=10×0.05×0.5×32⇒F=0.216 N

subject: Physics
solution:
Torque produced in a moving coil galvanometer is, τ=BINAsinϕ.

Let torsional constant of the suspension wire be C, then Cθ=BINAsin90°

⇒C= BINA θ=0.01×10×10-3×100×2×10-40.05

=4×10-5 N-m/rad.

Therefore, x=4.

subject: Physics
solution:
The magnetic force on a moving charged particle is given by F→=qv→×B→. Therefore, we know that the magnetic force is perpendicular to the magnetic field. Therefore, the resulting acceleration will also be perpendicular to the magnetic field. As the scalar product of two mutually perpendicular vectors is zero,

⇒a→·B→=0 ⇒αi^-4j^·2i^+3j^=0

⇒2α-12=0⇒α=6

subject: Physics
solution:
The magnetic field in between because of each will be in opposite direction
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text {in between }} & =\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi x} \hat{j}-\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi(2 d-x)}(-\hat{j}) \\
& =\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{2 d-x}\right](\hat{j})
\end{aligned}
$$
at $\quad x=d, \quad B_{\text {in between }}=0$
for $\quad x < d, \quad B_{\text {in between }}=(\hat{j})$
for $\quad x>d, \quad B_{\text {in between }}=(-\hat{j})$
towards $\mathrm{x}$ net magnetic field will add up and direction will be $(-\hat{\mathrm{j}})$
towards $\mathrm{x}^{\prime}$ net magnetic field will add up and direction will be $(\hat{\mathrm{j}})$

subject: Physics
solution:
Energy gained due to potential difference by charged particle:

KE=qV

Relation between momentum & kinetic energy:

p=2mKE

Now, radius of the circular path under magnetic field will be given by, 

R=mvqB=pqB=2m(KE)qB=2mqVqB

Therefore,

R∝m⇒m∝R2

⇒m1 m2=R1R22

subject: Physics
solution:
For a magnetic dipole placed in a uniform magnetic field, the torque is given by τ→=M→×B→, and potential energy U is given as,

U=-M→·B→=-MBcosθ

When M→ is in the same direction as B→ then τ→=0 and U is minimum = -MB as θ=0o.⇒ Stable equilibrium is (b).

When M→ the opposite direction of B→, then τ→=0 and U is max = +MB
As, θ=180o ⇒Unstable equilibrium in (d)

subject: Physics
solution:
Let the number of turns per unit radial width be $n = \dfrac{N}{r_2 - r_1}$.

Consider an elemental ring of radius $r$ and width $dr$. The number of turns in this element is $dN = n \, dr$.

The magnetic moment of this elemental ring is $dM = I \, dN \, A = I \left( \dfrac{N}{r_2 - r_1} \, dr \right) (\pi r^2)$.

Integrating from $r_1$ to $r_2$ to find the total magnetic moment $M$:

$M = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \dfrac{I \pi N}{r_2 - r_1} r^2 \, dr = \dfrac{I \pi N}{3(r_2 - r_1)} (r_2^3 - r_1^3)$

Substituting the given values: $I = 20 \times 10^{-3}$ A, $N = 200$, $r_1 = 0.03$ m, and $r_2 = 0.06$ m:

$M = \dfrac{20 \times 10^{-3} \times \pi \times 200}{3(0.06 - 0.03)} \left( (0.06)^3 - (0.03)^3 \right)$

$M = \dfrac{4\pi}{0.09} (216 \times 10^{-6} - 27 \times 10^{-6})$

$M = \dfrac{4\pi}{0.09} \times 189 \times 10^{-6} = 4\pi \times 2100 \times 10^{-6}$

$M = 84\pi \times 10^{-4} \approx 84 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-4} = 263.76 \times 10^{-4} \approx 2.64 \times 10^{-2}$ A.m$^2$

Comparing with $\alpha \times 10^{-2}$ A.m$^2$, we get $\alpha = 2.64$.

Answer: $2.64$

Here, side of the triangle, $l=4.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$, current, $\mathrm{I}=1 \mathrm{~A}$ magnetic field at the centre of the triangle ' $O$ ' $B=$ ? From figure, $\tan 60^{\circ}=\sqrt{3}=\frac{1}{2 d}$ $$ \Rightarrow d=\frac{l}{2 \sqrt{3}}=\left(\frac{4.5 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \sqrt{3}}\right) \mathrm{m} $$
Magnetic field, $B=\frac{\mu_0 i}{4 \pi d}\left(\cos \theta_1+\cos \theta_2\right)$ Putting value of $\mu=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}$ and $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ we will get net magenetic field $=3 \times B=4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^2$

solution:
Direction of magnetic field due to all six sides will be in the same direction, therefore we can write 

B=6μ0I4πrsin30°+sin30°, where r=distance of the sides from the centre.

=610-7×4π3π3×3

=72×10-7 T

Hence, x=72.

subject: Physics
solution:
The direction of the magnetic field at the observation point due to the current carrying conductor is determined by the right-hand cork screw rule.

From the given diagram, it is clear that as the conductors carry current in the mutually opposite direction, the direction of the magnetic fields produced by them at point P will be the same.

Hence, the required magnetic field at P due to the conductors can be calculated as follows:

B=2×μ0i2πa=2×4π×10-7×102π×52×10-2 T=160×10-6 T=160 μT
